9FMQ-D4P: Born in Aberdeen, Scotland, John William Landrum crossed the Atlantic to settle in St. Anne's Parish in Essex County, Virginia. He kept ties to England, where records still placed him in 1696, but Virginia became his home. He married three times over the course of his long life and died in Essex, where his family put down lasting colonial roots.
9S1J-6WB: Mary Carr began her life in Wiltshire, England, where she was baptized at Warminster and possibly fathered by a Thomas Carr. Like many of her generation, she made the journey across the ocean and ended her days far from home at Houstons Corner in Caroline County, Virginia, having lived more than eighty years between the old country and the new.
G574-14R: A native of the British Isles, William Harrison arrived in Virginia around 1638 and immigrated permanently by 1649, settling along the colony's tidewater. He rose to the rank of captain and lived to a remarkable old age, dying past one hundred years in Charles City County. Family tradition long celebrated his extraordinary longevity as much as his service.
GMRK-XLS: One of Virginia's prominent early planters, William Thornton arrived from England before 1646 and settled in Petsworth Parish of Gloucester County. He steadily acquired land along the Rappahannock and served his parish faithfully as a vestryman for nearly three decades. Through his many descendants, his line would reach into America's first families, counting Presidents James Madison and Zachary Taylor among his great-great-grandchildren.
KL4H-RWP: John Ladd was born in the Kentish village of Swingfield, England, and joined the wave of settlers bound for the New World, arriving in Virginia by 1653. He made his life in Henrico County on the colonial frontier, where he died in 1680, an early English planter who helped establish the family in Virginia soil.
KPQ6-2X2: Born in colonial Virginia, William Ogilvie Jr. moved south to Granville County, North Carolina, where he was recorded in the 1790 census. He answered the call to arms during the Revolution in 1780 and served again in the War of 1812. His long life of military service and migration finally came to a close in Williamson County, Tennessee.
L1W2-FV7: Joseph Adams came to New England as a boy in 1638, immigrating from Kingweston, Somerset, to the young settlement of Braintree in the Massachusetts Bay Colony. He earned his living as a farmer, maltster, and millwright, served his town as constable, and held the rank of captain. He is remembered above all as the great-grandfather of President John Adams.
L2ZY-BLV: Thomas Andrews left England as a young man, sailing into Virginia in January 1685 aboard the ship Richard and Elizabeth under an indenture of four years. He built a life in Henrico County, where he gave court testimony, secured a land grant of nearly 400 acres in 1704, and left a will before his death in 1732, an immigrant servant who became a Virginia landowner.
L5B4-5KS: Henry Bagley Sr. was born in Sussex County, Virginia, and later joined the southward migration into North Carolina. He performed military service there in 1782 and was settled in Chatham County by the turn of the century, raising his family in the Carolina backcountry where he died in 1802.
L6GZ-KHD: Edward Garrett was born in New Kent County, Virginia, and made his way south, receiving a grant in South Carolina's Ninety-Six District by 1767. He took up residence in the Spartanburg area and served during the Revolution, a service later honored by the Sons of the American Revolution. He died in Laurens County, having helped settle the South Carolina upcountry.
L8SL-6YS: Hugh McWhorter Sr. was born in County Armagh, Ireland, where he likely worked as a linen merchant before joining the Scots-Irish migration to America. A Presbyterian, he settled in Pencader Hundred, New Castle County, Delaware, around 1730, where he prospered as a linen merchant and farmer until his death in 1749.
LBK9-RLM: William Harris came to Virginia as a lieutenant in the English army and was granted vast tracts of land along Stafford County and Neabsco Creek. His life was marked by hardship: a 1681 letter records that he had been captured by Indians and held for ransom for more than a year. He died in 1698 and was remembered on his carved gravestone as a good soldier and kind neighbor.
LCJM-K4K: Born in Leiden, Holland, to the Pilgrim community in exile, Isaac Robinson sailed to New England in 1631 and helped settle Scituate and later Barnstable. He served as a deputy to the Plymouth Colony Court and kept an inn at Falmouth. A man of conscience, he was disfranchised for thirteen years for befriending the persecuted Quakers, and he lived to the great age of ninety-four.
LDB1-JFN: Jean Pierre Mallott was a French Huguenot refugee who fled France and found shelter for a time in Mannheim, Germany, where he was born in 1658. As a child his family crossed to New Amsterdam in the early 1660s. He settled in Piscataway Township, New Jersey, where he made his living as a homesteader and blacksmith until his death in 1703.
LHFR-CXT: Born in Charleston around 1690 to French Huguenot parents, Samuel Prioleau became one of the colonial city's leading citizens, working as a silversmith, jeweler, merchant, and planter, and holding the rank of colonel. He sat for a portrait by Henrietta Johnston, the earliest known woman artist in America, leaving a rare glimpse of life in early Charleston.
LJGP-Z7S: John Tucker was born in Salisbury in the Massachusetts Bay Colony in 1664 and bore the title of captain. Records also tie him to Westmoreland County, Virginia, hinting at a life that spanned colonies. He died at Gloucester on the Massachusetts coast in 1749, an early New England colonist whose story survives mostly in scattered records.
LKQB-LF8: Little is documented of Isaac Odom's life, but the record that endures is one of service: in 1781 he answered the call for militia duty in South Carolina during the Revolutionary War, taking his place among the ordinary patriots who helped win American independence.
LRWY-C31: Cuthbert Harrison was born in colonial Virginia in 1712 and carried the title of captain. He lived out his days in Prince William County, where he died in 1771. Though few records of his life survive, he belonged to a prominent Virginia family of the colonial era.
LTB4-VTX: Johann Peter Kinder was born in Dudweiler, in the Saarland region of Germany, in 1710 and emigrated to colonial America. He settled on the Virginia frontier in Augusta County, where his life was cut short in 1749. After his death, the parish court ordered his orphaned children, including young Peter and his sisters, to be bound out and cared for.
LVYC-5SX: Joseph Landrum Jr. was born in Essex County, Virginia, and trained as a doctor. He joined the steady westward migration of his generation, moving first to Orange County, North Carolina, and eventually on toward Indiana. His life traced the expanding frontier of the young nation before his death in 1800.
LXW3-J9D: Josiah Singletary spent his entire life in Bladen County, North Carolina, where census records track him faithfully from 1790 onward. His later years brought military service in the 1830s and 1840s. A lifelong North Carolinian, he remained rooted in the same county for his eighty years.
LYFC-R9P: Born near Ludwigshafen in the German Rhineland, Josephus Brunner farmed in the Schifferstadt region before emigrating from Rotterdam to America. He arrived at Philadelphia in September 1729 aboard the ship Allen and eventually settled in Frederick County, Maryland, where he established a home he named Schifferstadt after his German roots.
LZ6V-WY3: Joseph Jolly was born in Charleston, South Carolina, around 1718 and built his life in the colony's backcountry, securing hundreds of acres along Thickety Creek in what became Union County. He served in the military during the Revolution in 1777 and left behind a will and estate when he died around 1788, a patriarch of the South Carolina upcountry.
LZGQ-17W: Bonaventure Thériot, known as 'Venture,' was born around 1641 at Port-Royal in French Acadia and spent his life as a laboureur, tending a modest farm with his wife Jeanne Boudrot. Censuses recorded their cattle, sheep, and growing family of daughters. He lived a long Acadian life, dying past ninety at Grand-Pré in Nova Scotia.
M3KW-4NF: Frances Buckner was born in Old Rappahannock County, Virginia, and became the wife of a Thornton, living as a widow in Brunswick Parish of King George County in her later years. Her 1743 will, written while sick and weak in body but sound of mind, carefully provided for her grandchildren, leaving a tender record of a colonial Virginia matriarch.
MJ7D-PJ6: Simon Hadley was born at Moate in County Westmeath, Ireland, and in 1712 became the first of the Hadley family to emigrate to America, bringing his wife and children to Chester County, Pennsylvania. He later lived in New Castle County, Delaware, part of the great Scots-Irish movement into the colonies, and died in Pennsylvania in 1756.
PQFR-R34: Martha Corwin was born in Ipswich in the Massachusetts Bay Colony in 1632, among the first generation of New England-born colonists. She later moved to Southold on Long Island, New York, where she lived out her days, dying in 1698 after a life that spanned two of the earliest English colonies in America.
